US to West's energy watchdog: scrap net zero focus or we'll quit
data.description:
PARIS, Feb 19 (Reuters) - U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright gave the International Energy Agency on Thursday a one-year deadline to scrap its support of goals to reduce energy emissions to net zero or risk losing the United States as a member.

European countries played down the threat at the agency's biennial meeting and restated their commitment to pursuing cleaner fuel.

In 2015 the U.S. and nearly 200 other countries signed the Paris Accords, an international pledge to limit global warming by burning less oil, gas and coal, with a goal of reaching net zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050.

"There has been such a group mentality, 10 years invested in a destructive illusion of net zero by 2050, that the U.S. will use all the pressure we have to get the IEA to eventually, in the next year or so, move away from this agenda," Wright said.

BORN OF THE 1970S OIL SUPPLY CRISIS

The Paris-based IEA was formed after the 1970s oil supply crisis and provides research and data to the U.S. and other industrialised governments to guide energy policy.

The U.S. has paid about $6 million (5.10 million euros) per year in IEA dues, out of a total agency budget of $22 million.

U.S. President Donald Trump has rejected international efforts to tackle global warming and sought to unfetter the development of fossil fuels and stymie the rollout of renewable energy.

He has also repeatedly clashed with Europe and sought to minimise U.S. financial contributions to international organisations.

Wright said many countries have agreed in private with the U.S. stance to move away from net zero goals and continue to increase production and consumption of fossil fuels.

"We are certainly seeing a lot of nations, at least privately, talking about wanting to become competitive again, wanting to re-industrialise their countries, wanting to have strong militaries," Wright said.

However, he added some politicians, mostly in Europe, were unlikely to publicly backtrack on their green stances.

"A number of the European nations have staked their political platforms and frankly, their desire to be relevant in some area in the world on a net zero agenda. Only the cold, hard reality, the uprising of people and voting out political parties can change things," Wright said.

LONG-TERM GOAL TO END DEPENDENCE ON FOSSIL FUEL IMPORTS

French Finance Minister Roland Lescure was among those who played down the U.S. threat.

"The worst is never certain. I spoke with Chris Wright, I am convinced we have enough common subjects to work on together, including nuclear energy," Lescure said.

The host reinforced France's commitment to clean energy, and said the goal was to electrify its industry to rely less on oil and gas.

"France and Europe's strategic and structural answer is electrification. We of course still have gas needs, notably for the industrial sector, and I'm very happy the U.S. can meet that need, but objectively the long-term goal is to exit a dependence on imported fossil energy that is still too great," Lescure said.

Deputy Prime Minister for the Netherlands Sophie Hermans, who chaired the IEA event, said a large majority of countries expressed their desire to build more domestic clean power and rely less on foreign imports.

"Of course, I see how hard it is to achieve these ambitions. But that's not a reason for me to leave. No. I will roll up my sleeves and work harder," Hermans said.

IEA Director Fatih Birol declined to comment on the mandate from the U.S. to remove the net zero scenario from its annual World Energy Outlook forecast, but said its data was respected globally as reliable.

(1 euro = $1.1772)

(Reporting by America Hernandez and Forrest Crellin, editing by Inti Landauro, Alexandra Hudson and Barbara Lewis)

By Forrest Crellin and America Hernandez

Vale Base Metals sells majority stake in Thompson Nickel Belt to Canadian-led consortium
data.description:
SAO PAULO/TORONTO, Feb 19 (Reuters) - Vale Base Metals has agreed to sell its majority stake in the Thompson Nickel Belt mine in Manitoba to a consortium of buyers, a move that would lead to the creation of a new nickel company in Canada.

The consortium of buyers includes Toronto-based exploration company Exiro Minerals, private equity firm Orion Resources Partners, and Canada Growth Fund. Brazilian mining firm Vale will retain an 18.9% in the new company, which is named Exiro Nickel, and has signed a five-year off-take agreement with it.

The new consortium will invest $200 million to revive the mine, which Vale has had under strategic review since last year as the price of nickel fell to a five-year low due to higher supply and low demand.

"We still have 20 years' worth of profitable nickel in front of us," said Shastri Ramnath, the CEO of Exiro Minerals. Ramnath, who has been named chief executive of Exiro Nickel, added that the new company has to be able to produce nickel when prices are low and remain competitive.

Vale is one of the world's largest producers of nickel, which is used in the production of electric vehicles and other goods. The company is targeting nickel production of 175,000 to 200,000 tons in 2026.

"This $200 million is not in our pocket; the money is flowing into delivering the competitiveness of this operation going forward," said Shaun Usmar, the CEO of Vale Base Metals. He added that the company is standing by its new owners so that they are not burdened with the legacy liabilities.

The Canadian government officially considers nickel a critical mineral. G7 countries, including Canada, are racing to secure critical minerals such as copper and nickel in an effort to break the chokehold of countries like China and Indonesia on production of these metals.

(Reporting by Isabel Teles in Sao Paulo and Divya Rajagopal in Toronto; Editing by David Goodman and Paul Simao)

By Isabel Teles and Divya Rajagopal

data.title: Why the Prospect of War With Iran Is Boosting Oil Prices -- Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is up 1.8% at $71.60 a barrel.

--European benchmark gas is up 6.4% to 33.48 euros a megawatt hour.

--Gold futures are up 0.6% at $5,036.90 a troy ounce.

--LME three-month copper futures are down 0.3% to $12,818.50 a metric ton.


TOP STORY:

Why the Prospect of War With Iran Is Boosting Oil Prices

That's roughly how much petroleum flows through the Strait of Hormuz, a deep channel between Iran and Oman, each day, equivalent to almost a fifth of global oil demand.

Disrupting tanker traffic or shutting the strait altogether is one way Iran might seek to retaliate against a possible American strike, analysts say, which is why oil prices have jumped alongside the U.S. military deployment in the region. Earlier this week, state-affiliated media in Iran said the strait would partially close for "security precautions."


OTHER STORIES:

Crude Glut at Sea Keeps Traders Guessing as Prices Hold on Geopolitical Risk

Oil markets are wrestling with a disconnect that leaves traders questioning the outlook for crude: a large projected global surplus is colliding with surprisingly resilient prices.

Goldman Sachs estimates that the market ran a surplus of roughly 1.5 million barrels a day in 2025. Yet that hasn't translated into a sustained price decline so far this year, with crude oil futures buoyed by geopolitical risk premiums.

A key factor is the physical location of the surplus. Much of the extra crude hasn't built up in storage hubs tied to benchmark pricing but instead as sanctioned crude lingering at sea, with tankers loaded with Russian, Iranian and Venezuelan barrels swelling inventories afloat.

--

Repsol Lifts Dividend, Maintains Buyback Pace

Spanish energy company Repsol maintained the pace of its share buyback and raised its dividend for 2026.

The company said it plans to buy back 350 million euros ($412.4 million) of shares over the first half of 2026-keeping the pace steady with the buyback it announced in July. The company will also lift its 2026 dividend by 7.8% to 1.051 euros a share, it said.

--

Cenovus 4Q Profit Jumps on Higher Production

Cenovus Energy posted higher profit in the fourth quarter, thanks to greater oil output from its operations.

The Canadian integrated oil and natural gas company posted on Thursday higher net income of 934 million Canadian dollars ($682 million), or C$0.50 a share, up from C$146 million, or C$0.07 a share, in the comparable quarter a year ago.

--

State AGs Sue Trump Administration Over Clean-Energy Funding Cuts

Attorneys general from 13 states are suing the Trump administration for ending billions in funding for renewable-energy projects in mostly Democratic-led states.

The lawsuit, filed Wednesday in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California, says that the U.S. Energy Department and the Office of Management and Budget shouldn't be able to end funding that received bipartisan congressional approval.

--

Nestle to Shed Ice-Cream Business in Shake-Up

Nestle said it would offload its remaining ice-cream business and pull back from a raft of brands as part of a broader plan to revive the packaged-food giant's fortunes.

The maker of KitKat chocolate bars and Nescafe coffee is pursuing a turnaround under new Chief Executive Philipp Navratil after a string of setbacks, weaker-than-expected results and management upheaval in recent years.

--

CenterPoint Energy 4Q Profit Climbs as Expansion Efforts Accelerate

CenterPoint Energy reported a higher profit in the fourth quarter, as it moved to expedite efforts to invest in its energy capabilities and add load.

The Houston-based utility on Thursday posted net income of $264 million, or 40 cents a share, compared with $248 million, or 38 cents a share, in the same quarter a year ago.

--

Rio Tinto CEO Says Resolution Project Primary Focus For U.S. Expansion

Rio Tinto will engage with Washington on various opportunities as the Trump administration seeks to bolster America's mining industry, but is primarily focused in the U.S. on advancing the Resolution copper project it is developing with BHP Group in Arizona, Chief Executive Simon Trott said.

Speaking in an interview following the miner's annual results, Trott said Rio Tinto views the U.S. as an attractive place to invest and appreciates the Trump administration's show of support for the giant Resolution development.


MARKET TALKS:

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Gain Ahead of Storage Data -- Market Talk

0917 ET - U.S. natural gas futures pick up after declining the previous two sessions. A cold spell across the Midwest and eastern U.S. is expected to lift demand at the weekend and into early next week. The EIA's weekly storage report at 10:30 a.m. ET is expected to show a near-normal withdrawal, leaving the inventory deficit to the five-year average intact. "However, next week's EIA report is expected to be much smaller than normal due to much warmer-than-normal temperatures over the U.S. this week," NatGasWeather.com says in a note. Nymex gas is up 1.9% at $3.067/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Oil Futures Add to Gains on U.S.-Iran Risk Premium -- Market Talk

0915 ET - Crude futures are higher with traders seeing rising risk of a U.S. attack on Iran as it continues its military buildup in the Middle East. While more talks on Iran's nuclear program were held this week, and some progress was said to be made, an oil market concern is that military action could prompt a closure of the Strait of Hormuz, choking off crude shipments. "Iran fears dominate the market and front-end sellers have vanished," Scott Shelton of TP ICAP says in a note. WTI is up 2.2% at $66.59 a barrel and Brent is 1.9% higher at $71.68. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

Satellite Interference in Iran Could Indicate Anti-Air Defense Measures, Kpler Says -- Market Talk

1013 GMT - Renewed satellite interference off Iran's Assaluyeh complex--the country's largest petrochemical hub--has skewed vessel positions, briefly clustering ships into a single zone and distorting the maritime picture, according to Kpler. "These interferences typically occur when anti-air defense measures are activated," says Yui Torikata, senior crude analyst at the data provider. Kpler says it had deployed countermeasures in advance to safeguard port-call accuracy amid rising regional tensions. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Repsol's Earnings Beat Lowered Expectations -- Market Talk

0944 GMT - Repsol's fourth-quarter earnings were slightly better than expected, UBS analysts write. However, expectations for the Spanish energy company had fallen after its update in January. Net income in its upstream unit was 5% above consensus while its industrial unit came in 8% ahead, the analysts write. Its customer unit was 3% below consensus, they say. Shares rise 1.9% to 17.96 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

European Gas Prices Soar As U.S.-Iran Tensions Raise Alarm Over LNG Flows -- Market Talk

0820 GMT - European natural-gas prices climb in early trading, extending the previous session's gains as traders fear any escalation between the U.S. and Iran could disrupt LNG flows in the region. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract is up 3.5% to 32.60 euros a megawatt-hour after settling more than 5% higher on Wednesday. Gas markets are increasingly worried about shipments through the Strait of Hormuz--especially from Qatar, the second-largest exporter globally. "These supply risks are building at a time when EU gas storage continues to tighten, with it falling closer towards 30% full," ING analysts say. Still, forecasts of warmer weather across Europe, steady gas flows despite outages in Norway and stronger renewable output in Germany are easing pressure, limiting price gains. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Extends Gains As U.S.-Iran Concerns Mount

0803 GMT - Oil prices extend the previous session's gains as the market grows increasingly worried about potential U.S. action against Iran. Brent crude rises 0.9% to $70.98 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.7% to $65 a barrel after climbing more than 4% on Wednesday. Tensions between the two nations remain high, with Washington assembling the most air power in the region since the 2003 invasion of Iraq. "With a deal looking increasingly difficult to reach, it also means it will be more challenging to find a route to de-escalation, especially following the US military build-up we have seen in the region," ING analysts say. "And if de-escalation is not possible, the key question will then be what type of action the U.S. takes and how Iran responds to this." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

02-19-26 1059ET

data.title: Five countries have committed troops to international security force for Gaza, ISF commander says
Booking Slips on Wall Street After Outlook Deemed Disappointing
data.description:
For FY 2025, Booking Holdings said it generated adjusted net income of $7.44bn, up 22% from 2024, representing FY adjusted EPS of $228.06 (+22%).

Reported earnings fell 8% to $5.40bn, or EPS of $165.57 (-4%).

Annual revenue came in at $26.9bn, up 13% (+10% at constant exchange rates), while gross bookings rose 12% over the year to $186.1bn.

Adjusted EBITDA totaled $9.94bn for the year (+20%), while free cash flow reached $9.1bn for the year, up 15%.

The board also raised the quarterly dividend by 9.4% to $10.50 per share, payable on March 31, 2026, to shareholders of record as of March 6.

"These results illustrate the strength of our platform and the discipline of our execution," said Glenn Fogel, CEO of Booking Holdings, also highlighting progress in integrating generative AI and savings delivered by the transformation program, which will help support investments in 2026.

For 2026, the group expects revenue and bookings growth at a pace in the low double digits, slightly above its long-term trajectory.

Adjusted EPS growth is expected to be around 15% (mid-teens).

Analysts divided

Following the release, Bernstein reaffirmed its "market perform" rating on Booking Holdings, with its target price unchanged at $5,407. The broker noted, however, that the 2026 outlook looks "a bit disappointing" due to new investments and a lack of aggressive share buybacks.

The note said that despite fundamentals viewed as solid and "no immediate AI-related threat," management's comments on the potential disruption from AI did "not fully reassure the market," leaving the stock without a clear near-term catalyst.

Wedbush, meanwhile, reiterated its "outperform" rating on the stock but cut its target price to $5,300 from $5,500. The broker also cited a "mixed" outlook, with an EBITDA target up 12% at the midpoint, below initial expectations.

data.title: Trump says US will give UN money to make it stronger, more viable
data.description:
WASHINGTON, Feb 19 (Reuters) - The United States will give the United Nations money to strengthen it and help make it viable, President Donald Trump told the opening meeting of his Board of Peace on Thursday.

The U.S. is the biggest contributor to the U.N. budget, but under the Trump administration it has refused to make mandatory payments to regular and peacekeeping budgets, and slashed voluntary funding to U.N. agencies with their own budgets.

"We're going to strengthen up the United Nations. We're going to make sure its facilities are good. They need help ...we're going to help them money-wise, and we're going to make sure the United Nations is viable," said Trump.

"I think the United Nations has great potential, really great potential. It has not lived up to (that) potential."

(Reporting by Trevor Hunnicutt; Writing by David Ljunggren; Editing by Lisa Shumaker and Philippa Fletcher)

data.title: ABB India Limited Reports Earnings Results for the Fourth Quarter and Full Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
ABB India Limited reported earnings results for the fourth quarter and full year ended December 31, 2025. For the fourth quarter, the company reported revenue was INR 36,333.4 million compared to INR 34,515.5 million a year ago. Net income was INR 4,328.5 million compared to INR 5,284.1 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 20.5 compared to INR 25.1 a year ago. Diluted loss per share from continuing operations was INR 0.07 compared to INR 0.17 a year ago. Basic earnings per share was INR 20.43 compared to INR 24.93 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share was INR 20.43 compared to INR 24.93 a year ago.
For the full year, revenue was INR 135,550.9 million compared to INR 125,417.1 million a year ago. Net income was INR 16,682.6 million compared to INR 18,716.4 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 78.78 compared to INR 88.46 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was INR 78.78 compared to INR 88.46 a year ago. Basic earnings per share was INR 78.73 compared to INR 88.32 a year ago. Diluted earnings per share was INR 78.73 compared to INR 88.32 a year ago.
